description: This figure presents simulated evolutions of microstructure and dislocation density distribution on the YZ surface in low-alloy steel under varying deformation conditions using an inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D cellular automaton model. The three panels depict: (a) transient strain rate increase from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at 0.2 strain and 950°C; (b) the same strain rate jump at 0.4 strain and 950°C; and (c) temperature decrease from 1050°C to 950°C in the strain range of 0.4–0.5 at 0.01 s⁻¹. For each condition, the left column shows microstructural maps where white grains represent the unrecrystallized matrix, green grains denote first-stage DRX grains, and red grains indicate second-stage DRX grains, with DRX fractions explicitly labeled (e.g., first: 28.3%; second: 10.1% at 0.4 strain in panel a). The right column displays corresponding dislocation density distributions with color scales ranging from 0.001–2.000×10¹⁴ m⁻² for (a) and (b), and 0.01–3.5×10¹³ m⁻² for (c), revealing higher dislocation densities at grain boundaries compared to grain interiors. The images demonstrate that second-stage DRX grains nucleate preferentially at matrix grain boundaries due to higher distortion energy and progressively refine both the matrix and first-stage DRX grains as deformation proceeds, providing direct evidence for the dislocation-density-difference driven refinement mechanism responsible for stress softening under transient Z-parameter increases.
